data.body:
=== Growth prior-art research (how high-commit-volume git projects bound growth) ===

rebar's shape (append-only 1-commit-per-event branch, readers use worktree, clones sync by
union-merge) == a package-registry git index. That class hit this at scale; findings:

CONVERGENT INDUSTRY ANSWER = move the hot/high-churn data OFF shared git history onto HTTP/CDN:
- crates.io index: squashed history periodically (every 3-6 mo / at ~50-100k commits; archived
  old history to a separate crates.io-index-archive repo; deleting old branches matters because
  the server deltas master only against master). Team called shallow/squash "only temporary
  solutions"; real fix = sparse HTTP index (RFC 2789). git-as-high-commit-log deemed wrong tool.
- Homebrew 4.0.0: abandoned git taps, moved metadata to JSON over formulae.brew.sh; shallow
  clone was BANNED (server-hostile: incremental fetch into shallow recomputes trees).
- CocoaPods: Specs.git -> CDN. Go modules: HTTP proxy + Merkle-tile checksum log (never git).

CONSUMER-SIDE git tools (shallow, partial clone blob:none/tree:0, commit-graph, sparse-index,
git maintenance): reduce CLIENT cost only; NONE shrink the authoritative server store; treeless
partial clone RAISES server pack-objects CPU ~4x. Buys time, not a bound.

PRODUCER-SIDE (actually shrink the store):
- Periodic squash/re-root (crates.io) — shrinks; breaks pinned SHAs; needs archive + cadence bot.
- git replace --graft + filter-repo re-root — shrinks; rewrites ALL later commit SHAs; force-push
  + re-clone; disables commit-graph while grafts exist. filter-repo author calls periodic
  truncation "a bad usecase" but supports it.
- git-annex `forget` — THE closest analog: prunes a UNION-MERGED metadata branch's history;
  designed to survive UNCOORDINATED squash because union-merge re-converges different squash SHAs
  automatically (branch carries CONTENT not SHA-bound identity). Caveat: all clones need a
  supporting version; breaks if trust is bound to commit SHAs.
- Sapling/Mononoke segmented changelog (lazy history: don't shrink, stop transferring) + Dolt
  prolly-trees (size scales with changes not versions) — both require replacing git's object
  model, not layering on it.

VERDICT: (1) if a central read surface is acceptable -> serve current worktree state over
HTTP/CDN, history becomes internal/audit. (2) if distributed union-merge sync is non-negotiable
(rebar's case) -> periodic union-merge-SAFE squash/forget (crates.io cadence + git-annex
convergence), PROVIDED events don't bind trust to commit SHAs. (3) per-entity refs / lazy history
bound growth but replace the substrate.

FOR REBAR SPECIFICALLY: growth is 85% .bridge_state (a REGENERABLE cache). The industry pattern
for high-churn derived cache is NOT to keep it in shared history — but operator says it's
required, so the open question is whether its CURRENT state (required) can be retained without
its full HISTORY (219MB bindings.json/987 + 155MB prev_snapshot/4450), given the reconciler's
3-way-merge-baseline + binding-lifecycle needs. Understand those needs FIRST.

Full cited report in the session transcript (crates.io RFC 2789, git-annex forget docs, Homebrew
4.0.0, nesbitt.io "package managers keep using git as a database").
